pattern: Trailing zeros in n! come entirely from factors of 10, which require pairs of 2 and 5; since 2s vastly outnumber 5s, the count equals the total number of times 5 divides into 1 through n.
hints: Every trailing zero needs both a factor of 2 and a factor of 5; which of those two primes is scarcer in n!?
Count how many numbers from 1 to n are divisible by 5, then by 25, then 125, and so on — each contributes extra factors of 5.
Repeatedly divide n by 5 and accumulate the quotients; stop when the quotient reaches zero.

edge_inputs.description: n=5, first factorial with a trailing zero

stress.description: random n values across the full constraint range
